channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: America’s Enterprising Spirit Is Booming After Decades-Long Slump
item.description: Hundreds of thousands of people are creating companies each year, ending a decades-long drought.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.media:credit: Kayla Wolf for The New York Times
item.media:description: With things slow at her job and with the help of A.I., Cassidy Winkler started her own boutique Pilates business, Revel Method Pilates.

item.title: Cooler Inflation May Not Be Enough to Stave Off Fed Rate Increases
item.description: The Federal Reserve is expected to hold rates steady at its July meeting, but officials have not written off the possibility of a move soon after.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, chairman of the Federal Reserve, appeared before the House Financial Services Committee this week.

item.title: Trump Brings Full Weight of Government to Bolster False Election Claims
item.description: Intelligence agencies provided the White House with a trove of declassified documents that President Trump cited on Thursday as evidence of election vulnerabilities.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Nick Corasaniti and Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times

item.title: Trump’s Obsession With Relitigating the 2020 Election Carries a Cost for Democracy
item.description: In demanding steps to address the integrity of voting, President Trump persisted in relitigating his 2020 election defeat while finding ways to cast doubt on the 2026 outcome.
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump seen on a screen inside the White House briefing room as he addressed the nation on Thursday night.

item.title: Ontario Wildfires Burn On, and New Ones Spring Up
item.description: Nearly 200 fires continued to devastate swaths of the province, prompting evacuations in at least 15 communities.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Cole Burston/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Smoke was visible from the observation deck of the CN Tower in Toronto on Thursday.

item.title: Meta in Talks to Lease Computing Power to Anthropic in Potential $10 Billion Deal
item.description: A deal would underline how scarce computing power is for artificial intelligence development, and could create a new business for Meta.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Dustin Chambers for The New York Times
item.media:description: A data center owned by Meta in Georgia. A deal with Anthropic could potentially alleviate pressure on Meta from investors, who have questioned how much it has been spending on data centers.

item.title: Xi Jinping of China Pitches ‘Openness’ in Push to Shape the Path of A.I.
item.description: Xi Jinping said that the development of artificial intelligence systems should be a “symphony of global collaboration.”
item.dc:creator: Steve Lohr
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Ng Han Guan
item.media:description: Xi Jinping, China’s leader, spoke at the start of the World AI Conference in Shanghai on Friday.

item.title: Big Tech Firms Like Oracle Turn to Bonds to Finance A.I. Data Centers
item.description: Risk is rising as big tech companies like Oracle — the ultimate financial source of the Ellison media empire — need to turn to the bond market for staggering sums to finance data centers.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.media:credit: Jason Hoffman

item.title: Stocks Sink on Anxiety About Tech and A.I. Spending
item.description: A sell-off in Asia led by chipmakers spilled into Europe and U.S. markets on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Brendan Mcdermid/Reuters

item.title: Taking Out the Trash: These Old Ladies Are Cleaning Up Cape Cod
item.description: The Old Ladies Against Underwater Garbage estimate they’ve removed 6,000 pounds of refuse from ponds on Cape Cod. “It’s the best feeling,” one said.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley and Billy Hickey
item.media:credit: Billy Hickey for The New York Times

item.title: How Andy Burnham, ‘King of the North,’ Conquered U.K. Politics
item.description: A plain-spoken politician from a modest background in northwest England, Mr. Burnham became Labour leader — and Britain’s incoming prime minister — on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:credit: Jon Super/Associated Press
item.media:description: Andy Burnham in Ashton-in-Makerfield, England, in June. Mr. Burnham is on the verge of becoming prime minister.

item.title: Iran-U.S. Talks: How 4 Negotiators Would Approach Diplomacy
item.description: First step: Agree on a goal.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran in June. The likelihood of an easing of tension between the United States and Iran looks distant at the moment, but there are small signals that diplomacy could still be possible.

item.title: Dubai Migrant Worker Layoffs Reveal Costs of Middle East War
item.description: The Persian Gulf city attracted migrant workers with the promise of opportunity. Many now say the war has brought job losses and salary cuts.
item.dc:creator: Anjana Sankar and Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Natalie Naccache for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Satwa area of Dubai in April. Migrant workers in the city have faced job losses or cuts since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.

item.title: Cyclospora Linked to Taylor Farms Lettuce Sent to Taco Bell
item.description: Federal officials worked with Michigan investigators to trace the outbreak to iceberg lettuce, which may also have gone to other vendors.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett, Alice Callahan and Caroline Hopkins Legaspi
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: Cyclosporiasis cases have been linked to iceberg lettuce that Taylor Farms supplied to Taco Bell.

item.title: To Win Their Races, Black Politicians Confront a New Landscape
item.description: With the Voting Rights Act weakened, Black representation will depend less on Black voters and more on broad, multiracial appeal or on ideological outsider campaigns.
item.dc:creator: Bernard Mokam and Clyde McGrady
item.media:credit: Mustafa Hussain for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The idea that Black candidates are only electable in Black districts is patently false,” said Representative Lauren Underwood, who represents a largely white district.

item.title: Republicans in North Carolina Try to Reduce Early Voting on Sundays and on Campuses
item.description: Voting-rights activists said the changes are a blatant attempt by G.O.P. leaders to make it harder for Black voters and students, who tend to vote for Democrats, to cast ballots this fall.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Jonathan Drake/Reuters
item.media:description: Republican changes to early voting locations in North Carolina have prompted more than a dozen disputes between state and local election officials.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From a Maine Debate That Showed Replacing Platner Isn’t Easy
item.description: Maine Democrats vying to become the state’s new Senate nominee met, and mostly agreed, at a debate filled with halting answers and stilted deliveries.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Ryan David Brown for The New York Times
item.media:description: The first hour of the debate featured four of the top Democratic candidates for Senate in Maine: from left, Shenna Bellows, Troy Jackson, Dr. Nirav Shah and Jordan Wood.

item.title: The World Cup, in Photos
item.description: See some of the best images from the world’s most popular sporting event.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Judges Ask Congress for Power to Fix Crumbling Courthouses
item.description: Undrinkable water. Leaks. Mold. Federal courthouses need billions of dollars in repairs, and judges say the General Services Administration is a bad landlord.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Inside the modernist, steel-and-glass exterior of the Everett McKinley Dirksen U.S. Courthouse in Chicago, judges say that longstanding maintenance problems are interfering with the performance of their duties.

item.title: How Companies Have Abandoned Their Climate Goals and Let Themselves Off the Hook
item.description: Big business made big promises about saving the planet. Following through hasn’t been easy.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles

item.title: My 5 Favorite Places for Art in Venice
item.description: Our critic Jason Farago shares what you shouldn’t miss in a city full of secrets and surprises.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago
item.category: Palazzo Grimani di Santa Maria Formosa (Venice, Italy, Museum)
item.media:credit: Clockwise from left: Fine Art Images/Heritage Images, via Getty Images; Susan Wright for The New York Times, and Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times

item.title: Why Trump Can’t Leave the Smithsonian Alone
item.description: The Trump White House and its history police want us all to live in their chosen bubble, immune from instability or perceived disorder.
item.dc:creator: David W. Blight

item.title: Can Democrats Finally Understand the Latino Voter in an Era of Mass Deportations?
item.description: For decades, Democrats have misunderstood Latino voters. Trump’s deportation campaign offers a chance for them to get it right.
item.dc:creator: Andrea R. Flores
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Tam Stockton for The New York Times

item.title: Greek Life
item.description: We look at the place of Homer’s epic “Odyssey” in our cultural lives.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton

item.title: What Do You Want to Know About New York Times Journalism?
item.description: New York Times editors and reporters want to answer your questions about how we work and why we make the decisions that we do.
item.dc:creator: Megan DiTrolio
item.media:credit: September Dawn Bottoms/The New York Times
item.media:description: We want to answer your questions about our process and work.

item.title: The History of the Carriage Horses in Central Park
item.description: After 165 years, the tourist ritual — which now costs $77.22 for the first 20 minutes — may be coming to an end. Here’s a tour through the archives.
item.dc:creator: John Leland
item.category: American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals
item.media:credit: Neal Boenzi/The New York Times
item.media:description: The carriage ride through the park has been a rite of passage for visitors to the city for ages.
